patient.chief_complaint: My kid suddenly started having really bad worries and weird habits after a sore throat, and it’s getting worse fast.

volunteer: All of a sudden, he started washing his hands over and over and can’t stop
He has scary thoughts that something bad will happen if he doesn’t do certain routines
He got really irritable and has big meltdowns over small things
This started within a couple weeks after a bad sore throat and fever
He’s having trouble sleeping because he feels like he has to do things “just right”
if_asked: He has sudden little jerky movements and blinking that he says he can’t control
He’s more anxious when we separate, like at school drop-off
He’s been avoiding school and says he feels “unsafe” there
His handwriting suddenly got messy and he’s struggling to finish classwork
He’s been peeing more often and sometimes says he has to go right now, but it doesn’t burn
He keeps asking for reassurance again and again, even after we answer
He’s been more sensitive to sounds and clothing tags
He had strep throat before when he was younger, and this feels like another “strep episode” happened right before the behavior change
hidden: He started refusing certain foods because they feel “contaminated,” and he’s eating less than usual
He avoids touching doorknobs and won’t sit on certain chairs because they feel “dirty”
He takes a long time to get dressed because clothes have to feel “even” on both sides
He’s been complaining of stomachaches on school mornings
He’s been more clumsy than usual and drops things more often
He has brief moments where he seems “checked out” because he’s stuck doing a mental routine, but he’s awake and can answer if you interrupt him
resistant: He sometimes checks his private parts or adjusts his underwear repeatedly because it feels “not right,” and he’s embarrassed about it
He has had a couple of accidents at school because he’s too anxious to ask to use the bathroom
misleading: We think it might be autism because he suddenly can’t handle noises and routines
We’re worried it could be seizures because of the blinking and jerks
We wonder if it’s just ADHD because he can’t focus and his schoolwork is falling apart
noise: He has a mild runny nose this week
He gets occasional headaches after a long day
He had a small scrape on his knee from playing outside

progressive_reveal.symptom: This all began very suddenly within about 1–2 weeks after a bad sore throat with fever.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about timing and whether anything happened right before the behavior change (illness, stress, school changes).

progressive_reveal.symptom: He has new blinking/face movements and small jerks he says he can’t control.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks specifically about tics, unusual movements, or sounds.

progressive_reveal.symptom: He’s peeing much more often and has strong urgency, but no burning.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about bathroom changes, accidents, or urinary symptoms.

progressive_reveal.symptom: His handwriting suddenly got much worse and he’s struggling to finish schoolwork.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about school performance changes, fine motor tasks, and handwriting.

progressive_reveal.symptom: He’s eating less because certain foods feel “contaminated.”
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about appetite, weight change, picky eating, or fears around food.

progressive_reveal.symptom: He repeatedly adjusts his underwear/checks his private area because it feels “not right,” and he’s embarrassed.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks gently about any behaviors that feel embarrassing, private routines, or repeated checking.

general_concerns: Thinks PANDAS is contagious and worries he could give it to his classmates.
Believes his sudden tics and worries mean he is 'going crazy' and that it will never get better.
Thinks antibiotics should be taken anytime his behaviors flare, even if he doesn’t have a sore throat or fever.
Assumes a negative rapid strep test always rules out strep-related problems and that further testing is unnecessary.
Believes sugar, food dyes, or screen time are the main cause and that avoiding them will cure the condition without medical care.
economic_concerns: Family worries that repeated specialist visits and lab tests will be too expensive and may not be covered by insurance.
Parent is concerned about missing work for frequent appointments and school meetings.
Thinks therapy (CBT/exposure work) will be long-term and unaffordable compared with 'just getting antibiotics.'

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s parent bringing your 9-year-old son. Be cooperative but vague; start with: “After a bad sore throat and fever, he suddenly got intense worries and weird habits, getting worse fast.” If not interrupted, volunteer: repetitive handwashing, scary ‘something bad will happen’ thoughts, irritability/meltdowns, trouble sleeping because things must be “just right,” and that symptoms began within 1–2 weeks after the throat illness.

Only if asked, add: blinking/jerky movements he can’t control, separation anxiety and school avoidance (“unsafe”), messy handwriting and slower work, urinary frequency/urgency without burning, constant reassurance-seeking, sensitivity to sounds/tags, prior strep history.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: food “contamination” avoidance, doorknob/chair avoidance, dressing symmetry rituals, stomachaches on school mornings, clumsiness, brief ‘checked out’ mental routines.

Be reluctant/embarrassed to disclose unless directly asked: underwear/genital checking/adjusting and bathroom accidents at school.

Express misconceptions: worry about autism, seizures, or ADHD. Emphasize cost concerns and preference for “antibiotics over long therapy.” If clinician shows empathy, soften and share more details.

medications: Methylphenidate ER 18 mg PO each morning
Melatonin 1 mg PO nightly as needed for sleep
confounders: Pre-existing ADHD with stimulant use (can worsen tics/anxiety and mimic behavioral changes)
Recent psychosocial stressor (school transition with increased separation anxiety)
Intermittent motor tics predating current episode (raises possibility of Tourette syndrome)
Negative rapid strep test despite positive throat culture (timing/collection issues can obscure infectious trigger)
Sleep deprivation (worsens irritability, compulsions, and attention)

correct_diagnosis.primary: PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal infections)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute rheumatic fever with neuropsychiatric symptoms (Sydenham chorea spectrum)
Primary OCD/tic disorder with coincidental streptococcal infection
required_evidence: Pediatric onset (prepubertal) with abrupt/episodic onset of OCD and/or tics
Temporal association with Group A Streptococcus infection (positive throat test/culture or rising streptococcal antibody titers)
Neurologic abnormalities or associated features often seen in PANDAS (e.g., motor hyperactivity, choreiform movements, emotional lability, anxiety, urinary frequency, sleep disturbance)
Symptoms not better explained by another neurologic/medical disorder

expected_lab_results.Rapid Group A Strep (throat swab): May be positive if active pharyngitis is present; can be negative despite recent infection
expected_lab_results.Throat culture for Group A Strep: If rapid test is negative but suspicion remains, culture may be positive; otherwise negative if no active carriage/infection
expected_lab_results.Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer: Often elevated or rising on paired titers consistent with recent GAS infection (single titer may be normal early)
expected_lab_results.Anti-DNase B: Often elevated or rising on paired titers; may be more sensitive than ASO for some presentations
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Usually normal or mildly elevated; not diagnostic for PANDAS
expected_lab_results.Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): Usually normal or mildly elevated; not diagnostic for PANDAS
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (CBC) - WBC: Often normal; may show mild leukocytosis if concurrent infection

medications: Cephalexin 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for 10 days (max 500 mg per dose) if GAS confirmed and no immediate-type penicillin allergy
Azithromycin 12 mg/kg PO once daily for 5 days (max 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg daily) if cephalosporin not appropriate or concern for beta-lactam allergy
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/fever (max 40 mg/kg/day) if symptomatic
Sertraline 12.5-25 mg PO daily (start low, titrate slowly) for moderate-severe OCD/anxiety if impairing and after risk/benefit discussion
non_pharmacological: Confirm GAS infection with rapid antigen test and/or throat culture; obtain ASO and anti-DNase B (prefer paired titers 2-4 weeks apart if initial negative/unclear)
Refer for CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP) for OCD; behavioral therapy for tics
Screen for suicidality, severe functional impairment, and safety at home/school; provide crisis resources if indicated
Coordinate care with pediatrics, child psychiatry/psychology, and neurology as needed; consider PANS/PANDAS specialty referral if severe/refractory
Avoid routine prophylactic antibiotics or immunomodulatory therapy in primary care unless under specialist guidance; consider if recurrent, severe cases after specialist evaluation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks to review symptom trajectory and lab results; earlier (48-72 hours) if worsening, medication adverse effects, or safety concerns

must_include: PANDAS is a clinical diagnosis characterized by sudden onset of OCD/tics with a temporal link to strep infection
Lab tests help document recent/active strep but do not alone confirm or exclude PANDAS
Discuss key differentials (PANS, primary OCD/tics, Sydenham chorea/ARF) and why they are considered
If strep is confirmed, treat with an appropriate antibiotic course and emphasize completing therapy
Behavioral therapy (CBT/ERP) is a cornerstone for OCD symptoms; meds may be added when impairment is significant
Set expectations: neuropsychiatric symptoms may improve gradually; monitor for relapses with infections
Review allergy history: rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate and whether it was immediate/severe; discuss antibiotic options accordingly
Discuss when to seek urgent care: suicidality, severe agitation, inability to eat/drink, severe neurologic symptoms, or rapid deterioration
Discuss potential medication side effects (antibiotics GI upset/rash; SSRIs activation, GI upset, black-box warning for suicidality monitoring)
Infection prevention: hand hygiene and prompt evaluation for sore throat/fever in the child and close contacts as appropriate
School support planning for acute symptom flares (reduced workload, accommodations)
Sleep hygiene and stress reduction strategies that can reduce tic/OCD exacerbations

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core history: abrupt onset OCD/tics, temporal relationship to GAS infection, course/relapses, functional impact, and screen for urgent safety concerns.
Elicit infection history/exposures (recent sore throat/fever, household strep), prior testing/treatment, and review key differentials (PANS triggers, Sydenham chorea/ARF symptoms, primary OCD/tics).
Required before prescribing: clarify reported rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate (timing, features, severity, mucosal involvement, anaphylaxis) to guide antibiotic choice.
Order tests to document recent/active GAS when clinically indicated (e.g., rapid strep/culture; consider ASO/anti-DNase B if not acute), and evaluate for ARF features if suggested by history/exam.
Use results to support/discount recent/active GAS; recognize labs support but do not confirm/exclude PANDAS.
Make a clinical diagnosis (suspected PANDAS vs alternatives) based on abrupt onset OCD/tics plus temporal association with GAS and exclusion of close mimics.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review interactions with current meds (e.g., SSRIs, stimulants) and planned antibiotics/SSRIs.
If GAS confirmed/suspected per testing/clinical picture, prescribe an appropriate antibiotic course considering allergy history and contraindications; emphasize completing therapy.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis limits of testing, differentials, treatment plan (antibiotics if GAS, CBT/ERP, possible meds), expectations, urgent return precautions, side effects, and lifestyle/school supports.
Arrange follow-up to reassess neuropsychiatric symptoms, review adherence/side effects, coordinate CBT/ERP and school accommodations, and monitor for relapse with infections.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety net.

nl_assertions: Agent clearly states PANDAS is a clinical diagnosis characterized by sudden onset of OCD/tics with a temporal link to streptococcal infection.
Agent explains that lab tests (rapid strep/culture and/or ASO/anti-DNase B) can support evidence of recent/active strep but do not alone confirm or exclude PANDAS.
Agent discusses key differentials (PANS, primary OCD/tics, Sydenham chorea/acute rheumatic fever) and provides brief rationale for considering/excluding each based on the presented features.

Agent asks about the onset pattern (abrupt vs gradual), symptom types (OCD, tics), and associated neuropsychiatric symptoms (anxiety, emotional lability, sleep change, urinary frequency, regression) without overdiagnosing.

Agent screens for urgent safety issues and gives explicit guidance on when to seek urgent care: suicidality, severe agitation, inability to eat/drink/dehydration, severe neurologic symptoms, or rapid deterioration.

Before any prescribing, agent performs a structured allergy history focusing on the reported rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate and whether it was immediate, severe, involved mucosa, or had systemic features.
Before any prescribing, agent considers contraindications relevant to the chosen medication(s) (e.g., history of severe beta-lactam reaction; SSRI cautions) and documents this check.
Agent performs a drug interaction check prior to prescribing when feasible, especially if SSRIs or other psychoactive medications are being considered.

If strep is confirmed, agent recommends an appropriate antibiotic course and emphasizes completing therapy; if strep is not confirmed, agent avoids unnecessary antibiotics and focuses on symptomatic/behavioral management and reassessment.

Agent identifies CBT with exposure/response prevention (ERP) as a cornerstone for OCD symptoms and notes medications (e.g., SSRIs) may be added when impairment is significant, with monitoring for activation and suicidality (black-box warning).
Agent sets expectations that neuropsychiatric symptoms may improve gradually and that relapses can occur with infections; advises monitoring and prompt evaluation for new sore throat/fever.

Agent educates on infection prevention (hand hygiene; timely evaluation/testing for sore throat/fever in child and close contacts as appropriate) and offers school support planning during flares (accommodations, reduced workload).
Agent provides sleep hygiene and stress reduction strategies as supportive measures that may reduce tic/OCD exacerbations.
Agent communicates in a reassuring, non-stigmatizing manner, checks understanding, and avoids definitive claims that antibiotics will immediately resolve neuropsychiatric symptoms.

minimal_paths.description: Abrupt onset OCD/tics with temporal link to documented/active strep; proceed with supportive labs, confirm safety checks, treat GAS, and initiate behavioral management.
steps: ASK: characterize abrupt onset OCD/tics, course, impairment; screen urgent safety
ASK: assess recent/active infection, exposures, and differential features (PANS triggers, ARF/Sydenham signs)
CHECK_ALLERGY: clarify amoxicillin-clavulanate rash severity and timing
ORDER_LAB: rapid strep/culture (and consider ASO/anti-DNase B if not acute) as clinically indicated
DIAGNOSE: suspected PANDAS with documented/active GAS; note differentials considered
CHECK_INTERACTION: review meds vs planned antibiotic/possible SSRI
PRESCRIBE: appropriate antibiotic for GAS consistent with allergy profile; counsel to complete course
EDUCATE: diagnosis explanation, labs limitations, CBT/ERP, expectations/relapse monitoring, side effects, urgent return precautions, lifestyle/school supports
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: reassess symptoms and coordinate CBT/ERP and school plan

minimal_paths.description: Abrupt onset OCD/tics but no evidence of current/recent GAS; avoid unnecessary antibiotics, emphasize CBT/ERP, monitor, and broaden differential (PANS/primary OCD/tics).
steps: ASK: characterize onset, symptoms, impairment; screen urgent safety
ASK: evaluate for PANS triggers and primary OCD/tics; assess ARF/Sydenham features
ORDER_LAB: strep testing if clinically indicated based on symptoms/exposure
DIAGNOSE: PANS vs primary OCD/tics (PANDAS less likely given lack of GAS evidence); document differentials
EDUCATE: explain testing limits, rationale for not using antibiotics now, CBT/ERP emphasis, expectations, urgent return precautions, lifestyle/school supports
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: close follow-up; consider mental health referral/SSRI discussion if severe

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders strep testing early (before full differential discussion) but still completes required history, explains lab limitations, and does not diagnose solely from titers.

If antibiotic is not prescribed (no GAS evidence), agent still performs allergy check when discussing contingency planning for future confirmed strep and documents it before any potential prescribing.
Agent discusses SSRI option for severe OCD; performs interaction check before any SSRI prescribing and includes black-box warning counseling and monitoring plan.
